abstract.t: This document defines an information modeling language designed to improve the rigor of Internet technical specifications. Existing data standards like ABNF, XSD, and JSON Schema define formal syntax for text and structured data. But while and establish goals for information modeling, adoption remains limited. JSON Abstract Data Notation (JADN) addresses this by defining a formal syntax for information using a uniform type structure that balances simplicity with extensibility and expressive power. The defining feature of any IM is equivalence across data formats, but unlike traditional IM and DM schema languages, a JADN schema is itself an information value. This ensures equivalence across schema representations, allowing authors to select or design a representation suitable for the application and target audience.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: begins: "There has been ongoing confusion about the differences between Information Models and Data Models for defining managed objects in network management." The confusion can be partially attributed to the absence of a definition of information itself, so this specification begins with two:

dl.dd: structured, processed data that has been given context, meaning, and semantics within a specific domain. It turns raw facts into usable knowledge by organizing data objects, their attributes, and their relationships, typically at a high level of abstraction, independent of physical storage protocols.
the reduction of uncertainty, measured as the statistical rarity of a message rather than its semantic meaning. It quantifies how much a data point reduces doubt, measured in bits.

section.t: Given a literal value, the information it conveys can be viewed as the questions it answers. An information model (IM) defines equivalence between messages in different formats, and a concise message such as an RFC 791 IP packet contains the identical information (both semantics: "What is the source address?" and its entropy: "32 bits") as an equivalent verbose message in JSON or XML format.
An implementable IM specification must also define Value, Literal, and the relationships between them:

dl.dd: the value of a variable (a typed unit of state) that represents information in a processing environment. In this specification values are displayed with strings in single quotes to distinguish them from similar-looking JSON literals.

a fixed sequence of bytes usable outside a processing environment. Also known as a Document, Message, File, Stream, or Lexical Value. Because documents are immutable they can be compared for equality and hashed or signed for integrity.

a definition of the possible content of a value or literal. An IM type has three properties: a value space, a literal space for each data format, and a small set of functions and relations including ordering of the value space and mappings from each literal space to the value space. This definition extends the XML Schema Datatype to information modeling by including the literal spaces and mappings for multiple data formats. Every value has a Type and every literal is an instance of Type.

the implementation of a variable in a processing environment. An object has a type, a current value, and the behavior defined for variables of that type. Objects are instances of Class. This is not specific to object-oriented programming languages: K&R C has an "int" type, and its implementation of int variables (the int class) has behavior such as addition and comparison in addition to the value space defined by the int type. JavaScript defines Boolean, String, Number, Array, Map and Set objects, while JSON confusingly uses "object" to refer only to the literal values of Map objects. In this specification object includes variables of all types as in JavaScript, not just Maps as in JSON.

a named equivalence mapping between values and literals, implemented as a set of encoding rules. More than one data format can be defined for the same media type, to reflect preferences for conciseness or style.
Two literals are equivalent if and only if they are the same type and are mapped to the same value.

section.name: Objectives
section.t: The objectives of JADN are:

li.t: The IM must be abstract, unambiguously defining information values at a high level that separates information sharing requirements from implementation details.

ul.li: Implementation details include programming language and data format.
The IM is a high-level definition of structured information that can be used as formal pseudocode in documentation and standards even if it is not used directly to process data.
Segregating mapping methods into encoding rules allows the schema to be abstract.
The encoding rules that define a data format must be bi-directionally unambiguous to ensure that equivalence is verifiable.

Readability for the widest possible audience is JADN's highest priority. Humans are the primary audience for IMs; DMs are machine-oriented and may be less suited to understanding and explaining structured information.

li.t: An IM schema must itself be an information value, to support representation in multiple formats.

ul.li: Presenting a schema in a format familiar to the audience aids understanding. Schema-specific formats (e.g., property tables, entity relationship diagrams) may be more understandable to some audiences than general data formats. This specification defines the normative JSON schema format, but also uses equivalent textual IDL examples for readability.

An IM schema can also be encoded in a general data format and can either accompany or be referenced by the data it defines. CBOR has an objective that "Data must be able to be decoded without a schema description". This refers to well-formedness but also includes "self-describing", referring to validity and tags. CBOR data that includes a JADN schema in CBOR format is both self-describing and more concise than repeating Item Validity data in every instance. A schema in CDDL format is not suitable for this purpose because it conflicts with CBOR's compact decoder objective.

li.t: The IM must unambiguously define equivalence between values used in most processing environments and literals in most data formats used in Internet standards.

ul.li: The schema must be composed of data types, not classes or objects, because only types define the mapping between external data and internal values.
Associative arrays (maps, dictionaries, hashes) are universally used in programming but are not defined as types in UML, ASN.1, or XSD.
Other common processing types such as sets, ordered maps and unordered lists must be available in the IM for use if needed.

li.t: The IM language should be both conceptually simple and easily extensible to promote integration with existing data modeling approaches.

ul.li: Simplicity includes both structural uniformity and composition using the minimum set of elements needed to be sufficiently comprehensive. Those elements should be mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.
In addition to its use as pseudocode, an IM serves as a single source of truth when generating multiple DMs. Simplicity promotes both uses.

An unlimited variety of formally-standardized, coalition-sponsored, and company-promoted DM languages are used in the Internet. Translation among DM languages is not necessarily lossless, but a structurally uniform IM language that covers the entire intersection and much of the union of their capabilities simplifies translation to, and reverse engineering from, DMs in those languages.

li.t: The IM language must be usable for modeling data directly, to address data formats where DM languages are non-existent or insufficiently capable.

ul.li: There is no standard DM language available to validate IP Packets.
JSON Schema does not define ordered maps, e.g., document sections indexed by both name and position such that front matter must appear before back matter, or unordered lists.

li.t: There is no requirement for values to be uniquely encoded. However:

ul.li: Encoding rules should define a canonical mapping from information to data, to facilitate equivalence verification and extend integrity and authentication mechanisms from data to the information it represents.

Encoding rules should define many-to-one mappings, where helpful, from data to information to support the Robustness Principle. Using an IM to validate incoming data against well-defined mappings to a schema is Virtuous Intolerance.

section.name: Examples
section.t: A JADN schema value and the values it defines are each representation-independent.

section.name: Schema
section.t: Example representations of a trivial GPS Coordinate type illustrate that a JADN schema is a value, that that value can be represented in multiple data formats, and that the value is identical regardless of format. The formats shown are:

ul.li: JSON data: the normative storage format for JADN schemas.
XML data: an XML format composed of JADN-defined elements, called XML Abstract Schema Definition (XASD) language.

Text: JADN Information Definition Language (IDL), an example domain-specific language (DSL). Because a JADN schema is an Abstract Syntax Graph (ASG), metaprogramming and projection editing DSL tooling is particularly applicable.
Property Tables: a widely-used data standards publication format, shown as Markdown source. Tables can also be represented in HTML, XML and office document data formats.

Entity Relationship Diagram: a diagram format widely used in database design, traditionally categorized into three detail levels - Conceptual, Logical and Physical. Information modeling defines a fourth Information level between Logical and Physical, expressing all the detail of a Physical diagram relevant to information values (e.g., data types, keys) without detail related to data transmission and storage.

figure.name: JADN XASD Format
figure.artwork: <?xml version="1.0" ?> <Schema version="2.0"> <Types> <Type name="Coordinate" type="Record">GPS Coordinate <Field fid="1" fname="latitude" type="Number" minInclusive="-90.0" maxInclusive="90.0">Latitude</Field> <Field fid="2" fname="longitude" type="Number" minExclusive="-180.0" maxInclusive="180.0">Longitude</Field> </Type> </Types> </Schema>

figure.name: JADN IDL Format
figure.artwork: Coordinate = Record // GPS Coordinate 1 latitude Number=[-90.0, 90.0] // Latitude 2 longitude Number=(-180.0, 180.0] // Longitude

figure.name: JADN Property Table, Markdown Format
figure.artwork:
**Type: Coordinate (Record):** GPS Coordinate
| ID | Name | Type | \# | Description | |----|---------------|------------------------|----|-------------| | 1 | **latitude** | Number=[-90.0, 90.0] | 1 | Latitude | | 2 | **longitude** | Number=(-180.0, 180.0] | 1 | Longitude |

section.name: Data
section.t: A single value of type Coordinate (for example 38.8895, -77.0352) is processed using an object containing two floating point variables, but details of object behavior aside from its value are immaterial. That value can be serialized using multiple dialects of XML, JSON, YAML, raw binary, CBOR, and others, and equivalence across formats can be seen by inspection:
XML:
JSON:
YAML:
Binary - 8 bytes, two IEEE 754 floats:
CBOR - 11 bytes, two floats:
section.sourcecode: 1. 82 # array(2) FA 421B8ED9 # primitive(1109102297) FA C29A1206 # primitive(3264877062)

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Types
section.t: A JADN schema is composed exclusively of types, not a mixture of types and other mechanisms variously called classes or objects in other data modeling approaches. Types are organized in schema packages where a package optionally defines metadata shared by all types, including a namespace to support cross-package type references using qualified names.
The top-level structure of a JADN Schema package is:
section.sourcecode: ["Schema", "Record", [], "Definition of a JADN package", [ [1, "meta", "Metadata", ["[0"], "Information about this package"], [2, "types", "ArrayOf", ["*Type", "s"], "Types defined in this package"] ]]
section.t: Every JADN Type definition has the identical five-member structure, which can be verified as being well-formed without validating content:

ol.li: TypeName: the name of the type being defined.
CoreType: The JADN built-in type of the type being defined.
TypeOptions: a set of zero or more TypeOption values applicable to CoreType (conditional).
Fields: an array of zero or more Item or Field definitions as applicable to CoreType (conditional).

section.t: This uniform structure makes a JADN schema capable yet easy to process. Currently-defined TypeOptions and FieldOptions provide significant expressive power, and unlimited space for new options enables easy extensibility.

JADN defines values and message syntax based on twelve Core types in three categories, based on well-known abstract concepts such as "integer", "sequence" and "map". These concepts are not exhaustively defined here because many other publications provide excellent definitions. However, this specification will attempt to describe them well enough that they can be readily recognized and distinguished from other abstractions with which they may be confused.
The JADN Core types are:

ul.li: Primitive: Five types where each instance is an atomic value. The Fields array is empty in primitive type definitions.

Compound: Five types where each instance is a collection of values. The Fields array is empty for unstructured compound types (ArrayOf, MapOf) and has the same five-member Field structure for all structured compound types (Array, Map, Record).

Union: Two types where each instance is selected from a set of possible values. Each Field is a three-member Item structure for the Enumerated type, and the five-member Field structure for the Choice type.

section.t: The Item structure for Enumerated types is:
The Field structure for Compound and Choice types is:

JADN types form a directed acyclic graph (DAG) from top-level root node(s) down to leaf nodes that are not further decomposed. Definitions can be arranged in hierarchical tiers for readability, and types can be referenced from multiple definitions. This differs from tree graphs where types are always acyclic and denormalized, and from mesh graphs that make no distinction between contained and referenced content. Cross-references (links / foreign keys) in a DAG support cyclic relationships where needed, but are the exception rather than the rule. Data must live somewhere, and only a model that distinguishes values from references specifies where in a document or message to find it.

section.name: Options
section.t: lists all TypeOptions and FieldOptions defined in this specification. Their meaning and usage is described under specific types in the following sections. Each option has a numeric ID, a name, and a value type. In the JSON data format options are serialized as a list of strings using Tagged String encoding for brevity. In tagged strings the Unicode codepoint of the first character is the option ID and the remaining characters are the string representation of its value. Tagged string tables highlight that options are enumerated, not arbitrarily-named attributes. Tagged string encoding can also be used for general data by defining tables similar to .

In the GPS Coordinate example, latitude has options represented in JSON format as ["y-90.0", "z90.0"]. From the first character "y" (U+0079 = ID 121) has name "minInclusive", the remaining characters are the value string "-90.0", and the type "BType" is a JADN-reserved keyword indicating that the value type is the base type of the field (Number). The value of the latitude options is {'minInclusive': -90.0, 'maxInclusive': 90.0}:

figure.name: Tagged String Attribute Format
figure.artwork: +----+-----------+ Tagged String TypeOption = "y-90.0" (String) | ID | Value | ID = 121 = U+0079 = lower case "y" +----+-----------+ Info: Name = "minInclusive", Value = -90.0 (Number)

section.t: The more verbose XASD representation: <type="Number" minInclusive="-90.0" maxInclusive="90.0"> is equivalent to the to the JSON representation because both are mapped to the same value.

figure.name: TypeOptions and FieldOptions
figure.artwork:
ID TypeOption Type ID Description ___ __________ _______ ___ __________________________________________ 61 id Boolean =: Fields are indexed by FieldId, not FieldName 42 valueType String *: Value for ArrayOf and MapOf is the named type 43 keyType String +: Key for MapOf is the named type 35 enum String #: Enumerated type derived from the named type 62 path String >: Pathname Enumerated derived from named type 37 pattern String %: String matches regular expression 117 default BType u: Default value 118 const BType v: Constant value 119 minExclusive BType w: minimum value, excluding bound 120 maxExclusive BType x: maximum value, excluding bound 121 minInclusive BType y: minimum value 122 maxInclusive BType z: maximum value 123 minLength Integer {: minimum char/byte count or collection count 125 maxLength Integer }: maximum char/byte count or collection count 113 unique Boolean q: ArrayOf instance is an OrderedSet 115 set Boolean s: ArrayOf instance is a Set 98 unordered Boolean b: ArrayOf instance is a Bag 111 ordered Boolean o: Map, MapOf, Record instance is an OrderedMap 67 combine Integer C: Choice instance is an untagged union 47 format Map /: Semantic validation keywords 116 tagString String t: Table for tagged-string serialization 97 abstract Boolean a: Type is not instantiable as a value 102 final Boolean f: Type cannot have subtypes 101 extends String e: Type is a superset of referenced type 114 restricts String r: Type is a subset of referenced type 65 attr Boolean A: Literal may be an XML attribute
ID FieldOption Type ID Description ___ __________ _______ ___ __________________________________________ 91 minOccurs Integer [: min cardinality, default = 1 93 maxOccurs Integer ]: max cardinality, default = 1 48 nillable Boolean 0: Instance may have null value 38 tagId Integer &: field that specifies tagged union type 60 dir Boolean <: flattened field type may be a collection 75 key Boolean K: field is the primary key for TypeName 76 link Boolean L: field links to an instance of FieldType

section.name: TypeOptions
section.t: Type options applicable to an individual CoreType are defined in the section for that type. Type options applicable to multiple CoreTypes are:

ul.li: extends, restricts, abstract, final: specifies a Type Inheritance shortcut.

li.t: format: specifies a set of semantic validation keywords from among those defined for CoreType. A keyword defines the value space for TypeName instances (validation) and the method for translating between values and literal representations (semantics). The same keyword may be defined for more than one CoreType, for example the /time keyword on a String type defines valid string values, and on the Integer type defines the translation between POSIX time and its literal representations.

This specification defines a set of semantic validation keywords. Keywords may also be defined in external namespaces (XSD, JSON Schema, or application-specific value spaces + translations) and imported into a package. This enables an IM's types to remain minimal, abstract and precise while linking them to an extensible set of precise representation details.

section.name: FieldOptions
section.t: TypeOptions apply to specific types, but FieldOptions apply to the individual fields of structured types independently of FieldType. The FieldOptions are:

ul.li: minOccurs, maxOccurs: specify the Multiplicity shortcut.

nillable: specifies that the field's value space includes the null value in addition to the values specified by its type. Every optional field without a default value is also nillable, but no required field without the nillable option may have a null value.

tagId: specifies a field within a structured type to use as the tag in a Tagged Choice field. The tagId option often references a Derived Enumeration field, allowing one or more tagged unions to select values based on the same tag.

dir: nested information values have a flattened equivalent, analogous to a recursive list of file pathnames. The dir option stops the flattening recursion, allowing the field to contain nested values, analogous to directories.
key: identifies a field as being an id (index or primary key) for a value of the defined type. Key values must be unique over the scope of an index.
link: identifies a field as being a cross-reference (link or foreign key) to instances of FieldType. The FieldType of the referenced key field is the type of the link value.

section.name: Primitive Types
section.t: A primitive type has no substructure, and specifies an unrestricted space of atomic values without regard to processing mechanisms or data format. Options applicable to each type limit the value space; if more than one option is present the value space is their intersection.
JADN defines the following primitive types:

ul.li: Boolean: a value from the set of two possible states.
Integer: a value from the discrete whole number line.
Number: a value from the continuous real number line.
String: a sequence of characters.
Binary: a sequence of octets.

section.t: Type options applicable to multiple Primitive CoreTypes are:

ul.li: const: value is fixed, equivalent to minInclusive = maxInclusive. Value contains zero bits of information.

li.t: default: when this type is an optional member of a Collection:

ul.li: encoding member values that have the default value omits literal members where possible.
decoding missing literal members populates member values with the default value.

li.t: Collections encoded as arrays omit trailing optional members but must preserve the positions of all collection members. Collections encoded as maps omit members with default values.
minInclusive, maxInclusive, minExclusive, maxExclusive: value range options. Each option value must be a member of the value space of CoreType. It is an error for:

ul.li: minInclusive and minExclusive to be specified in the same type.
maxInclusive and maxExclusive to be specified in the same type.
minInclusive to be greater than maxInclusive.
minInclusive to be greater than or equal to maxExclusive.
minExclusive to be greater than or equal to maxInclusive.
minExclusive to be greater than or equal to maxExclusive.

attr: specifies that a value should be encoded as an XML attribute when using an XML data format. Ignored by non-XML data formats, and may be ignored by specific XML formats. Elements are required for non-primitive content and preferred for primitive content. Attributes are preferred for descriptive data about content, e.g., image metadata, unique identifiers, units.

section.name: Boolean
section.t: A Boolean instance has one of two values, denoted true and false. Its value space is the set of those two values, representing a single bit of information. Its literal space typically includes many representations including {true, false}, {1, 0}, or {non-empty, empty} strings or collections. Each data format should designate a single canonical value-to-literal mapping, and as many literal-to-value mappings as make sense for the application. For example, JSON defines the 4-5-character literals true and false but a concise JSON data format could specify numeric literals 1 and 0 instead.
Options: const, default, format.

section.name: Integer
section.t: An Integer instance is a value from the ordered countably infinite space of discrete values {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...}.

An integer is often said to be a number with no fractional part. Integer and number are distinct types, both mathematically and computationally, because a countably infinite set of discrete values differs from a continuum. 1.01 has a fractional part, as does 1.00000000001, as does 1 + 10^-n for every non-infinite positive n. For this reason every decimal character string and every IEEE-754 floating point value that represents an integer, regardless of precision, also represents an infinite number of real values with a fractional part. It is impossible for the Number type to uniquely represent an integer without consuming infinite resources.

Options: const, default, format,
minInclusive, maxInclusive, minExclusive, maxExclusive.

section.name: Number
section.t: A Number instance is a value from the ordered uncountably infinite space of real numbers. This illustrates a fundamental difference between information and data modeling: JADN's Number value space includes all real numbers, while XSD's float value space is a subset of rational numbers and excludes all irrationals.

Options: const, default, format,
minInclusive, maxInclusive, minExclusive, maxExclusive.

section.name: String
section.t: A String instance is a sequence of characters from a character set.
These options apply to the String type:

ul.li: pattern: specifies an unanchored regular expression. XSD Appendix G discusses regular expression languages, but this specification does not constrain the language other than requiring expressions to include explicit anchors ("^" and "$") if needed.
minLength, maxLength: specifies the minimum and maximum number of characters.

section.t:
Options: const, default, format,
minInclusive, maxInclusive, minExclusive, maxExclusive,
pattern, minLength, maxLength.
Value range options are meaningful only if character set (encoding) or collation order are known.

section.name: Binary
section.t: A Binary instance is a sequence of octets.
These options apply to the Binary type:

ul.li: minLength, maxLength: specifies the minimum and maximum number of octets.

section.t:
Options: const, default, format,
minInclusive, maxInclusive, minExclusive, maxExclusive,
minLength, maxLength.

section.name: Collection Types
section.t: An IM collection is a collection of values and its equivalent literals. Every collection type has two components:

ul.li: a semantic type that characterizes the properties of a collection value within a processing environment, based on the MultiplicityElement.
a compound type that characterizes the literals used to represent a collection value.

section.t: These components are equally important, and any IM must specify both semantics and structure. A JADN CoreType is one of five structural compound types and may include a TypeOption to specify collection semantics.

Data modeling languages are generally unable to define the significance of collection order independently of structure, as discussed in Element order is always important in XML, except when it isn't. An IM that defines both semantics and structure avoids the limitations imposed by data modeling languages.

section.name: Collection Semantic Types
section.t: defines MultiplicityElement as specifying collection multiplicity (the valid number of values contained in the collection) and four semantic types based on two Boolean properties. Because UML does not support mapping types, JADN defines a third Boolean property to enable them:

dl.dd: if true then the collection is ordered. This implies that there is a mapping from positive integers to the values in the collection. If a collection is not multivalued, then the value for isOrdered has no semantic effect. If false then no assumptions can be made about the order of the values.
if true then the collection must be unique. That is, no two values in the collection may be equal. If a collection is not multivalued, then the value for isUnique has no semantic effect.

If true then the collection is associative. This means that there is a mapping from keys to the values of the collection, where each key is an immutable value of any type and keys must be unique within the collection. If false then values are not associated with keys.

section.t: Taken together, the isOrdered, isUnique, and isAssociative properties specify that a collection value is one of six semantic types. Because keys are unique the combination of isUnique=false and isAssociative=true is not used.

shows the name commonly used for each combination of collection properties, and the compound type(s) that can support that semantic type. Compound types without options (shown in bold) have either Sequence or Map semantics. But it is fairly common for data modelers to use other combinations such as using array literals with "id" fields to represent Map semantics.

section.name: Sequence
section.t: A Sequence is an ordered collection of values where a value can appear more than once and each value is mapped to a positive integer (its position). Sequences may be combined into a new sequence whose values are the concatenation (in order) of each of the sequences (in order). Adding or removing a value changes the positions of all following values.

section.name: Set
section.t: A Set is an unordered collection of values where no value appears more than once and no value has a position. Set operations include union, intersection, difference and symmetric difference. Coercing a set into a sequence yields a sequence of values in unspecified order.

section.name: OrderedSet
section.t: An OrderedSet is an ordered collection of values where no value appears more than once. Because values have a position they may be inserted or removed from the collection by either position or value. OrderedSet values may not be directly combined, but must be coerced into and then combined with either Set or Sequence, yielding different results.

section.name: Bag
section.t: A Bag (or ) is an unordered collection of values where values may appear more than once. A Bag can be instantiated efficiently as a set of unique values with each value mapped to its count. Two Bags are equal if they contain the same members each with the same count. A Sequence, Set, OrderedSet or Bag can be combined into a destination Bag by iterating over each value in the sources and incrementing the count of that value in the destination.

section.name: Map
section.t: A Map is a collection of key:value pairs where the keys are a Set. Each key and each value may be any type and keys must be immutable. Because Map keys must be unique, a collection of key:value pairs that supports duplicate keys may be modeled as a Set of Map values or a Map of Set values.

section.name: OrderedMap
section.t: An OrderedMap is a collection of key:value pairs where the keys are an OrderedSet. Each key and each value may be any type and keys must be immutable. Some programming languages define "order-preserving" mapping types. These are ordered only if two collections with the same members in a different order compare as unequal.

section.name: Compound Types
section.t: A JADN compound type specifies the data used to represent collection information.

ul.li: Structured compound types , and define the type of each member individually, by position, key, or both. Each Field defines the association between the identifier (position and/or key) and the field type.
Unstructured compound types and have uniform field types rather than individually-defined fields. ArrayOf is a generic collection structure that can represent any semantic type.
The minLength and maxLength options specify the minimum and maximum number of collection members. Member defaults may affect the length of literals that represent the collection.

section.name: Array
section.t: The Array type defines a collection of individually-typed members where each member has a position, commonly referred to as a struct:

ul.li: FieldId is the position of each member, numbered sequentially starting at 1.
FieldName is a comment that describes the member but does not exist in the collection value.
The value is a of members, each with a position and type.

section.t: Applications may refer to Array fields directly by position or define a mnemonic label to refer to each field. Because field names do not exist in Array values, applications may use FieldName or choose arbitrary position mnemonics without affecting interoperability.
Options: minLength, maxLength, format.

ul.li: The format option is a semantic validation keyword that defines the valid values of the struct and its literal representations.

section.name: Map
section.t: The Map type defines a collection of individually-typed members where each member has a key:

ul.li: FieldId is the numeric key of each member, arbitrary but unique within the collection.
FieldName is the string key of each member, following the $FieldName naming convention and unique within the collection.
By default the value is a of members, each with a key and type.

section.t: These options apply to the Map type:

ul.li: The id option specifies that members are identified only by FieldId and that FieldName does not exist in member values but may be used as a mnemonic label for FieldId.
The ordered option specifies that the value is an of members ordered by FieldId.
The tagString option references a translation table similar to that defines a collection of IDs, Names and Types that a data format can use to represent a Map value as a list of tagged strings.

section.t:
Options: minLength, maxLength,
id, ordered, tagString.

section.name: Record
section.t: The Record type defines a collection of individually-typed members where each member has both a position and a key:

ul.li: FieldId is the position of each member, numbered sequentially starting at 1.
FieldName is the string key of each member, following the $FieldName naming convention and unique within the collection.
By default the value is a of members, each with a position, key, and type.

section.t: Options: minLength, maxLength, ordered.

ul.li: The ordered option specifies that the value is an of members.

section.name: ArrayOf
section.t: The ArrayOf type defines a collection of members of the same type. The required valueType option specifies the type of all collection members. The semantic type depends on both collection options and the type specified by valueType.

Options: valueType (required),
minLength, maxLength,
unique, set, unordered,
ordered.

ul.li: If valueType is an Array, Map or Record type with no key field, ArrayOf specifies a of table rows, with the columns defined by valueType fields and the rows indexed by position. No collection semantic options are valid.

If valueType is an Array, Map or Record type with a key field, by default the value is a of table rows, with columns defined by valueType fields and the rows indexed by both key and position. The ordered option specifies that the value is an of of table rows where order is significant.
For all other valueTypes the value is a of values by default. A unique, set, or unordered option specifies that the value is an , , or respectively.

section.name: MapOf
section.t: The MapOf type defines a collection of members with the same key and value types. The required keyType option specifies the type of all member keys. The required valueType option specifies the type of all member values.

Options: keyType (required), valueType (required),
minLength, maxLength, ordered.

ul.li: The ordered option specifies that the value is an of members. This requires that keyType has a defined ordering, unlike the structured type where FieldId ensures that field order is always defined.

section.name: Union Types
section.t: A union type specifies a set of alternatives used to classify a value. Like Compound types, Enumerated and Tagged Union types have fields individually identified by tag, where the tag consists of an integer FieldID and a string ItemValue or FieldName, each of which is local to and unique within the type definition. Union types define a set of tags, types or both.

tr.td: Tagged union, a set of tag:type pairs
Untagged union, a logical combination of types

section.name: Enumerated
section.t: An Enumerated type defines an explicitly listed set of values, a vocabulary. It is formally a degenerate tagged union of unit type because it defines the tags of a tagged union with no additional information. In a full tagged union, the tag identifies the type of the following data. In an enum, the tag is the data.
Options: id.

JADN tags include both numeric ID and equivalent string value, allowing either to be used. The id option specifies that an Enumerated value is an item_id, otherwise it is an item_value that may be represented by the id depending on data format.

section.name: Tagged Choice
section.t: The Choice type without a combine option is a tagged union. Its value is a tag that selects a type from the specified set of fields and an instance of the selected type.
Options: id.

section.name: Untagged Choice
section.t: The Choice type with a combine option is an untagged union. The option value specifies the logical combination of field types used to classify validity:

ul.li: 1 (allOf): value must be an instance of all field types.
2 (anyOf): value must be an instance of any field type, tried in field order until a match succeeds.
3 (oneOf): value must be an instance of one field type and no others.
4 (diff): value must be an instance of the first field type and no others.

section.t: The anyOf and oneOf options classify the specific FieldType matched in addition to a Boolean valid/invalid result. For example, if a Choice(anyOf) type includes both a "PhoneType" Enumerated field (e.g., "Home", "Office", "Mobile") and a String field for other types, PhoneType must appear first, otherwise the value "Home" will be classified as an instance of String, not PhoneType. A Choice(oneOf) type with the same fields will always classify the value "Home" as invalid regardless of field order.
Options: combine.

section.name: Packages
section.t: The fundamental unit of a JADN schema is the Package. A package contains a collection of type definitions and optional metadata applicable to all definitions in the collection. Packages enable modularization, allowing large models to be composed of manageable subunits.

figure.name: JADN Schema Package
figure.artwork:
Schema = Record // Definition of a JADN package 1 meta Metadata optional // Information about this package 2 types ArrayOf(Type) set // Types defined in this package
Metadata = Map // Information about this package 1 package Namespace // Unique name/version of package 2 prefixes Prefixes optional // External reference qualifiers 3 roots Roots optional // Root(s) of the type graph 4 config Config optional // Configuration variables 5 jadn_version Namespace optional // JADN Metaschema namespace 6 version String{1..*} optional // Increment within pkg package 7 info Annotation optional // Descriptive info about package
Namespace = String /uri // Unique name of a package
Prefixes = ArrayOf(PxNs){1..*} set // Set of prefix,namespace pairs
PxNs = Array // Prefix for a namespace IRI 1 Prefix // px:: Namespace prefix string 2 key(Namespace) // ns:: Namespace IRI
Roots = ArrayOf(TypeName) set // Unreferenced type definitions
Config = Map{1..*} // Override JADN defaults 1 $MaxBinary Integer=[1,*]=(255) optional // Max octets 2 $MaxString Integer=[1,*]=(255) optional // Max characters 3 $Maxmembers Integer=[1,*]=(255) optional // Max collection cardinality 4 $Sys String{1..1}=(".") optional // TypeName system character 5 $TypeName String=("^[A-Z][-.A-Za-z0-9]{0,63}$") /regex optional 6 $FieldName String=("^[a-z][_A-Za-z0-9]{0,63}$") /regex optional 7 $Prefix String=("^([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9]{0,7})?$") /regex optional
TypeName = String{pattern="$TypeName"} // Name of a type FieldName = String{pattern="$FieldName"} // Name of a field Prefix = String{pattern="$Prefix"} // Namespace prefix TypeRef = String // Type Reference matching ($Prefix ':')? $TypeName
Annotation = Choice(3) 1 String // str:: Description string 2 Descriptors // desc:: Named attributes
Descriptors = Map{1..*} 1 title String{1..*} optional // A short identifier 2 description String{1..*} optional // A longer explanation 3 comment String{1..*} optional // A note 4 copyright String{1..*} optional // Copyright notice 5 license String{1..*} optional // SPDX licenseId of package 6 deprecated BString optional // Should not be used 7 default String{1..*} optional // Description of default 8 examples String{1..*} optional // Text examples 9 readOnly BString optional // JSON Schema API 10 writeOnly BString optional // JSON Schema API
BString = Enumerated // Boolean value strings 1 true 0 false

section.name: Package Metadata
section.t: Metadata includes functional values that affect schema processing and annotation values that describe the package without affecting function.
Functional metadata:

li.t: package: the namespace of this schema package, a URI that uniquely identifies the package and qualifies the names of its types. In an IM namespace, URI schemes, paths, file extensions and data formats are irrelevant. The URI does not identify documents, it is a query value used to resolve package references to one or more documents that represent the package. Package namespaces should follow versioning best practices to ensure robustness and maintainability.
Metadata is optional in a schema package, and if present "package" is its only required value.

prefix: a short alias used to qualify a local TypeName with a namespace URI. Prefixes are used in TypeRef values to reference types defined in other packages; JADN does not define a TypeRef syntax that uses the namespace URI directly.

Namespaces may also reference external resources of any kind, where user-defined types specify the reference syntax with or without a prefix. All namespace URIs are unique IDs; they may or may not be locators. A web server may return file not found or a document in any media type over a secure protocol when dereferencing any namespace URI.

t.em: Editors Note: Are non-JADN examples useful or distracting?

li.t: Example prefix values. The first two are JADN package namespaces, the rest identify other external resources, illustrating various naming and versioning approaches:

ul.li: jadn: the JADN version 2.0 metaschema URI.

acme: a user-defined JADN schema. The "example.com" organization is assumed to be authoritative for the namespace. "acme:Sprocket" references the Sprocket type defined in the package, which might be found on the web at https://www.example.com/im-schemas/widget39.jadn or on a local filesystem at ~/ProjectFoo/schemas/widgets/w39.jidl.

gj: the GeoJSON RFC specifies a JSON geospatial data interchange format in prose without including a machine-readable schema. Although the RFC can be referenced, its type definitions are not usable without translation to a supported IM or DM language.

x509: the ITU-T ASN.1 module defining an identity and attribute certificate framework. ASN.1 is an abstract schema definition language, and types defined within an ASN.1 module can be referenced using a prefix notation, e.g., "x509:TBSCertificate" or "x509:Validity".
jss: the JSON Schema core metaschema. The namespace URI has no file extension and dereferencing it returns the core metaschema JSON document.
xs: XML Schema resources. Dereferencing the URI returns an HTML document that links to multiple resources identified within the namespace.
qudt: the QUDT (Quantities, Units, Dimensions and Data Types) ontology. Dereferencing returns the ontology document in RDF/OWL format.

li.t: roots: list of unreferenced type names in the package. This is redundant, but is included so that applications are not required to build a dependency list. It also serves to flag types that may be undefined if listed in roots, or unneeded if not.

config: This specification recommends the default naming conventions and size limits shown in for schema-processing tools. Consistent naming conventions improve readability and size limits reduce attack surface, but tool defaults may not be appropriate for all applications. Config values override them with package-level defaults, and type definitions may specify individual size restrictions that override package and tool defaults.

By default a JSON Schema string type has no maximum size, based on the assumption that applications are well-behaved. JADN assumes that malicious actors may attempt to compromise receiving applications with unreasonably large data values, and recommends applying small limits (255 bytes / characters / collection members) to types that do not explicitly define one. Package defaults reduce the typing burden of enforcing application-appropriate limits.

ul.li: $MaxBinary: applies a default maxLength:n option to Binary types.
$MaxString: applies a default maxLength:n option to String types.
$MaxMembers: applies a default maxLength:n option to Compound types.
$Sys: sets the path separator character for TypeNames generated by shortcut processing.
$TypeName: sets the regex used to validate TypeName. The default allows Train-Case or PascalCase.
$FieldName: sets the regex used to validate FieldName. The default allows snake_case or camelCase.
$Prefix: sets the regex used to validate Prefix.
jadn_version: the JADN metaschema used to validate this package.

version: a string used to recognize the most recent iteration among the documents found when resolving the package namespace. It may be a serial number, timestamp or any format with an unambiguous sort order.

section.t: Annotation metadata:

t.em: Editors Note: Refactor Description to Annotation usable in Metadata and Type, with name ("copyright") and value string in Map format and just value. Unlike JSON Schema, all annotations, not just $comment, can be stripped at any time and cannot affect validation. All annotation

ul.li: title:
description:
comment:
copyright:
license:
deprecated, readOnly, writeOnly: (JSON boolean literal in String)
default, examples: (JSON literal in String)

section.name: Shortcuts
section.t: JADN defines a set of core definitions plus several shortcuts that make IM schemas more adaptable or less redundant. Shortcuts are syntactic sugar implemented using options that can be replaced with additional types without affecting functionality. Expanding shortcuts into core definitions simplifies serialization and validation code, and viewing the expanded/simplified schema may aid understanding of the IM.
The shortcuts and the options used to invoke them are:

ul.li: Anonymous Types: any TypeOption present in FieldOptions
Multiplicity: minOccurs, maxOccurs
Derived Enumerations: enum, path
Type Inheritance: extends, restricts, abstract, final

section.name: Anonymous Types
section.t: This shortcut allows fields within a structured type to be defined without a TypeName. Expanding the definition generates an explicitly path-named type for each anonymous field, moves all TypeOptions included in FieldOptions to the generated type, and replaces FieldType with the TypeName of the generated type. FieldType must be a non-structured CoreType, and any TypeOption included in FieldOptions must apply to FieldType.
Example: a structured type with anonymous fields:
section.sourcecode: Coordinate = Record // A GPS coordinate 1 latitude Number=[-90.0, 90.0] // lat: -90 to 90 degrees 2 longitude Number=(-180.0, 180.0] // long: -179.9999 to 180 degrees
section.t: Expanding the type generates two named types and replaces FieldType with their names. This requires a naming convention for generating path names as discussed in . Path components may be either field IDs ("Coordinate.1") or field names ("Coordinate.latitude"). Writing an IM using explicitly named types (e.g., "Latitude") avoids this shortcut.
section.sourcecode:
Coordinate = Record // A GPS coordinate 1 latitude Coordinate.latitude // lat: -90 to 90 degrees 2 longitude Coordinate.longitude // long: -179.9999 to 180 degrees
Coordinate.latitude = Number=[-90.0, 90.0] Coordinate.longitude = Number=(-180.0, 180.0]

section.name: Multiplicity
section.t: Fields may be defined to have multiple values of the same type using the minOccurs and maxOccurs FieldOptions. This supports multiplicity notation (1..*), which is universally used in diagram formats. Expanding this shortcut converts each field that can have more than one value to a separate path-named ArrayOf type, but leaves single-valued fields (maxOccurs=1 by default) unchanged. The default minOccurs=1 indicates a required field, while specifying minOccurs=0 indicates that the field is optional.
If an optional field instance with no members should be represented by an empty list rather than omitted entirely, the IM collection type uses an explicitly-named ArrayOf type in a required field.

section.name: Derived Enumerations
section.t: The enum and path options generate a list of items based on the fields of the type named in the option. When expanded, enum copies the field id and name directly from the named type, while path generates a recursive list of pathnames below the named type. The dir field option terminates recursion when expanding the path option and when flattening values from tree to list structures. Expanding replaces the shortcut Enumerated type with the explicit item list. These options avoid duplicating long field lists, saving space and eliminating potential inconsistency.
Using the GPS Coordinate example, an Enumerated type with the enum option expands to the same type with an explicit list of fields:
section.sourcecode:
Foo = Enumerated(Enum[Coordinate])
Foo = Enumerated 1 latitude 2 longitude
section.t: In the Coordinate type the path option yields the same result as enum because both fields are leaf types (Number). But if "latitude" were a hypothetical compound type with additional structure, the "path" result would be a list of pathnames:
section.sourcecode:
FakeCoordinate = Record // A GPS coordinate 1 latitude Stuff // lat: a hypothetical structure 2 longitude Longitude // long: -179.9999 to 180 degrees
Stuff = Record 1 bar/ MoreStuff // "dir" option stops path recursion 2 baz MoreStuff // otherwise continue to leaf values
MoreStuff = Record 1 qux Integer // a leaf type 2 xyzzy String // another leaf type
Longitude = Number=(-180.0, 180.0] // also a leaf type
==== Expanding the _path_ shortcut yields: =====
Foo = Enumerated(Path[FakeCoordinate])
Foo = Enumerated 1 latitude.bar 2 latitude.baz.qux 3 latitude.baz.xyzzy 4 longitude

section.name: Type Inheritance
section.t: Class inheritance is complex and dynamic because it addresses behavior. But because types define immutable value spaces, type inheritance consists of two basic set operations (union and intersection) that can be precomputed on a schema rather than being processed at runtime. The extends and restricts shortcuts perform these schema operations, saving space and improving robustness:

ul.li: extends: value space is a superset of the base type. If A extends B, A = A ∪ B (union), and every instance of B is also an instance of A.
restricts: value space is a subset of the base type. If A restricts B, A = A ∩ B (intersection), and every instance of A is also an instance of B.

section.t: Two other options are adopted from class inheritance for completeness:

ul.li: abstract: type cannot be instantiated as a value.
final: type cannot be extended or restricted.

section.t: Although the subset rule authoritatively defines inheritance for all types, it is not useful for primitive types because:

ul.li: a primitive value space is the universe of possible values and cannot be further extended
existing TypeOptions directly restrict primitive value spaces without designating a base type
the identical result can always be expressed using Choice to "extend" or "restrict" value spaces without designating a base type:

section.sourcecode:
Name1 = Choice(anyOf) // extend: 2915, a34c, D72F are valid. g16H isn't. 1 String{pattern="^[a-z0-9]$"} // a:: 2 String{pattern="^[A-Z0-9]$"} // b::
Name2 = Choice(allOf) // restrict: 2915 is valid. a34c, D72F, g16H aren't. 1 String{pattern="^[a-z0-9]$"} // a:: 2 String{pattern="^[A-Z0-9]$"} // b::
section.t: In practice inheritance is used to add or remove fields in structured compound types or modify multiplicity in any compound type. Because Array and Record fields are indexed by position, extending appends new fields at the end. Record field names must be unique, but Array field names are comments with no uniqueness requirement. Map fields are indexed by key, so extending Map types requires that both field id and name be unique, but position has no meaning or effect.
Examples:
section.sourcecode:
Entity = Record abstract // Base type, cannot be instantiated 1 id Integer 2 name String optional
Person = Record extends(Entity) // Add email address 1 email String /email optional // Person type has 1 id, 2 name, 3 email
AnonymousPerson = Record restricts(Person) // Prohibit name 2 name String [0]

section.name: Schema Serialization
section.t: ...non-normative examples...

section.name: Data Serialization
section.t: A JADN IM is based on several equivalence mechanisms that can be included in any data format. This enables a single IM to ... CBOR goals ...

ul.li: Indexing Record members by position or name
Enumerating collection members by numeric ID or string name / value
Defining string formats for non-string types

section.name: Semantic Validation Keywords
section.t: The format option specifies a set of semantic validation keywords applicable to a type. Each keyword is defined for one or more CoreTypes, and its semantics is that of the type's value. For example, the /timestamp keyword is defined for the String and Integer types, and comparing two String representations yields different results than comparing two Integer representations.
Each keyword definition has a name and value type, like each JADN TypeOption definition.

t.em: Editor's note - turn notes into format list

ul.li:
Note: convert to format /E:n
scale: an option value n specifies a fixed point exponential scale factor of 10^-n, as in SI unit prefixes. It supports exact integer-valued fractional quantities that eliminate floating-point rounding errors. For example, a dollar-denominated Integer currency value with scale=2 is the number of centi-dollars (cents), or with scale=5 it is the number of milli-cents.

section.t:
complex numbers, quaternions and tensors - struct and operations
time - Integer and Array (struct_tm) are different types with different semantics Integer time has units (seconds), range (/i64) and scale (/s3), Integer geologic time /mya keyword specifies units (single-ended million years ago), range (/u16) and scale (/s1 for 39.2 mya). /mya not defined for Number because 39.28469 mya is precision without accuracy for even the smallest (/f16 - half-precision) floating point representation. Precision effect of modeling discrete and continuous mathematical spaces as Integer and Number as computationally distinct types.
entailment: some formats may entail others - /mya entails unsigned but not range
JSON discussion of +/-0 and floating point NaNs, /f# specifies finite value space including specials, complete or simple captured in data format, not schema. Number with no /f# has no specials

format MUST have a Map value to support /mya and /u16 on same type - scale can be a format rather than a separate option type, define taggedString table, xasd can use composite taggedString single format attribute.

JADN defined keywords - xref. Define and import other keyword namespaces (XSD, JSON Schema, domain-specific (e.g., /mya). Must be unique to include with no prefix.
Human or AI skill to translate qudt into IM types/keywords.

section.name: Relationship to Ontologies

t.em: Ed Note: Raw bot output confuses IM and DM, rewrite for accuracy

section.t: There is significant overlap between information models and ontologies. They serve different purposes but the overlap allows interaction between them.

li.t: An information model defines the structure, relationships, and rules for a specific domain's data — focused on how information is organized and exchanged in a system. It's typically created for a particular application or standard (e.g., a healthcare system, a network management protocol).

ul.li: Concerned with data structure and semantics in a bounded context
Often tied to an implementation or exchange format (JSON, XML, UML class diagrams)
Examples: CIM (Common Information Model for power grids), HL7 FHIR, UML models

li.t: An ontology formally represents knowledge about a domain — the concepts, their properties, and the logical relationships between them. It's designed to be shared, reusable, and machine-reasoned over.

ul.li: Concerned with meaning and logic, not just structure
Built for interoperability across systems, enabling inference
Usually expressed in OWL, RDF, or Description Logics
Examples: Gene Ontology, SNOMED CT, schema.org

section.t: Key Differences at a Glance Dimension | Information Model | Ontology Primary goal | Structure data for exchange | Represent knowledge for reasoning Formality | Moderate (schema-level) | High (logic-based) Reusability | Often domain/system-specific | Designed for broad reuse Reasoning support | Limited | Built-in (inference, entailment) Typical users | Software engineers, architects | Knowledge engineers, AI/semantic web Expression language | UML, XSD, JSON Schema | OWL, RDF, Description Logic

The Overlap Both describe concepts and relationships in a domain. In practice, ontologies are often used to define the semantics of an information model — so an information model might say "a Patient has a birthDate field," while an ontology would say "Patient is a subclass of Person, and birthDate is a functional property with range xsd:date, and no Person can have more than one." A useful mental shortcut: information models answer "what shape is the data?" while ontologies answer "what does the data mean?"

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This memo includes no request to IANA.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: This document should not affect the security of the Internet.

front.title: XML Schema Definition Language (XSD) 1.1 Part 2: Datatypes
Element order is always important in XML, except when it isn't

section.name: Appendix 1
section.t: This becomes an Appendix
